data.title: Sweden's EQT AB eyes $2.6B takeover of Kakaku.com - report
data.description:
• Swedish private-capital group EQT AB (EQBBF) is exploring a potential takeover of the Tokyo-listed Japanese operator “Kakaku.com, according to a The japan times reported citing people familiar with the matter..
  • The deal would value Kakaku.com at about $2.6B–$2.7B, reflecting the stock’s recent rally after rumors surfaced. Kakaku traded about 10% higher on the Japanese stock exchange at press time.
  • The deal deliberations are ongoing and may not result in a bid, the  people said.
  • Kakaku.com runs a core price‑comparison portal, often branded as “Kakaku.com” or “Kakaku”) plus restaurant‑search and job‑board services, positioning it as a diversified Japanese internet‑services group. The company has recently expanded into online recruitment via its acquisition of engage, a job‑listing platform previously operated by en Inc.

data.title: Sony Financial shares fall as Sony Life probes dozens of alleged customer misconduct cases
data.description:
• Sony Financial Group shares fell after a report that its unit Sony Life Insurance is probing dozens of cases of alleged misconduct against customers.
  • The Tokyo-based insurer is investigating multiple incidents of financial wrongdoing, adding to a series of scandals that have shaken Japan’s insurance sector, Nikkei reported.
  • The incidents of fraud at Sony Life are estimated to span 20 to 30 cases, the report said.
  • Prudential Life Insurance has found the misconduct scandal to be a wide-ranging structural problem, the Japan-based insurer's president said Wednesday, adding that a freeze on new sales was needed to implement major reforms.

data.title: BE Semiconductor GAAP EPS of €0.65, revenue of €184.9M; gives Q2 outlook
data.description:
• BE Semiconductor press release (BESIY): Q1 GAAP EPS of €0.65.
  • Revenue of €184.9M (+28.3% Y/Y).
  • Orders of € 269.7 million were up 7.7% vs. Q4-25.
  • Net cash of € 103.3 million increased € 67.3 million, or 186.9%, vs. Q4-25 due to improved profitability and cash flow generation.
  • Q2 Outlook: Revenue expected to increase 30%-40% vs. the €184.9 million reported in Q1-26.
  • Gross margin expected to increase to a range of 64%-66% vs. the 63.5% realized in Q1-26.
  • Operating expenses expected to increase 0%-10% vs. the €53.5 million reported in Q1-26.

data.title: Sartorius Aktiengesellschaft Non-GAAP EPS of €1.20, revenue of €899.1M; reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Sartorius Aktiengesellschaft press release (SARTF): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €1.20.
  • Revenue of €899.1M (+1.8% Y/Y).
  • Underlying EBITDA1 margin of 29.7 percent.
  • Outlook: For the full year 2026, management continues to forecast Sartorius Group’s sales revenue growth in constant currencies to be between around 5 and 9 percent, including a contribution of approximately 1 percentage point from the MATTEK acquisition and US tariff surcharges.
  • The underlying EBITDA margin should increase to slightly above 30 percent due to volume and scale effects (PY: 29.7 percent).
  • The ratio of capital expenditures to sales revenue is expected to remain at a similar level to 2025 (12.5 percent).
  • The Bioprocess Solutions Division is anticipated to achieve sales revenue growth in constant currencies of around 6 to 10 percent, mainly driven by the consumables business, while the equipment business is expected to remain at least stable.
  • The underlying EBITDA margin should be slightly above 32 percent (PY: 31.7 percent).
  • For the Lab Products & Services Division, management forecasts sales revenue growth in constant currencies of around 2 to 6 percent, including a growth contribution from MATTEK of around 1.5 percentage points.

data.title: Sanofi beats Q1 estimates; reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Sanofi press release (SNY): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €1.88 beats by €0.09.
  • Revenue of €10.51B (+6.3% Y/Y) beats by €280M.
  • Pharma launches sales increased by 49.6%, reaching €1.2 billion, driven primarily by Ayvakit, ALTUVIIIO, and Sarclisa.
  • Dupixent sales increased by 30.8% to €4.2 billion, a strong start to 2026.
  • Vaccines sales increased by 2.1% to €1.3 billion, benefiting from Heplisav-B.

  • Guidance affirmed

    • In 2026, sales are expected to grow by a high single-digit percentage at CER. Business EPS at CER is expected to grow slightly faster than sales (before share buyback), delivering profitable growth.

data.title: Dassault Systemes Non-GAAP EPS of €0.30, revenue of €1.51B; gives Q2 and reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Dassault Systemes press release (DASTY): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €0.30.
  • Revenue of €1.51B (-3.8% Y/Y).
  • In the first quarter, ARR grew 6% year-over-year to reach €4.37 billion on a constant currency basis.
  • Dassault Systèmes’ net financial position totaled €2.40 billion as of March 31, 2026 compared to €1.79 billion last year.
  • Cash and cash equivalents totaled €4.87 billion as of March 31, 2026.
  • Cash flow from operations totaled €949 million, an increase of 17% compared to the first quarter of 2025.

data.title: Sanofi and Regeneron’s Dupixent becomes first biologic for kids under 12 with CSU
data.description:
Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron’s (REGN) Dupixent was approved in the U.S. as the first biologic medicine for young children with uncontrolled chronic spontaneous urticaria, the companies said on Wednesday. This expands the previous approval for Dupixent in adults and adolescents aged 12 years and older with CSU.

The approval for children aged two to 11 years with CSU who remain symptomatic despite H1 antihistamine treatment is based primarily on data from the LIBERTY-CUPID clinical study program.

CSU is a chronic skin disease that causes itch and hives that can be debilitating for young children, especially for those whose disease remains uncontrolled.

CSU marks the fifth disease driven in part by type 2 inflammation for which Dupixent is approved in children younger than 12 years of age. The safety results from all four CSU studies were generally consistent with the known safety profile of Dupixent in its approved dermatological indications, the company said.

In Study A, Study B, and Study C, the most common adverse reaction (≥2%) in the US Prescribing Information more frequently observed in patients on Dupixent compared to placebo was injection site reactions. No new adverse reactions were identified in children aged 2 to 11 years with CSU treated with Dupixent, Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron’s (REGN) added.

data.title: Ipsen S.A. reports Q1 results; reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Ipsen S.A. press release (IPSEY): Q1 Revenue of €1.07B (+16.5% Y/Y) misses by $130M.
  • Full-year guidance 2026 Ipsen is confirming its financial guidance for full-year 2026: • Total sales growth greater than 13.0%, at constant currency, assuming accelerated sales growth of the portfolio excluding Somatuline and the growth of Somatuline sales due to generic lanreotide challenges.
  • Based on the average level of exchange rates in March 2026, an adverse effect on total sales of around 1% of currencies is expected.
  • Core operating margin greater than 35.0% of total sales.

data.title: Teck Resources beats Q1 estimates; reaffirms FY26 production outlook
data.description:
• Teck Resources press release (TECK): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of C$1.75 beats by C$0.64.
  • Revenue of C$3.94B (+72.1% Y/Y) beats by C$720M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA1 of C$2.1 billion in Q1 2026 was C$1.2 billion or 125% higher than the same period last year.
  • Cash flow from operations of C$1.0 billion increased our net cash1 position by C$338 million at March 31, 2026.
  • QB copper sales volumes of 70,300 tonnes in Q1 2026 were a quarterly record and materially exceeded production volumes of 55,500 tonnes as inventory was drawn down.

  • Guidance

    • There are no changes to our previously disclosed guidance, which is outlined in summary below and our usual guidance tables, including 2027–2028 production guidance, can be found on pages 26–29 of Teck’s first quarter results for 2026 at the link below.
       
    2026 Guidance – Summary Current
    Production Guidance  
    Copper (000’s tonnes) 455 – 530
    Zinc (000’s tonnes) 410 – 460
    Refined zinc (000’s tonnes) 190 – 230
    Sales Guidance – Q2 2026  
    Red Dog zinc in concentrate sales (000’s tonnes) 30 – 40
    Unit Cost Guidance  
    Copper net cash unit costs (US$/lb.)1 1.85 – 2.20
    Zinc net cash unit costs (US$/lb.)1 0.65 – 0.75

data.title: Pathward maintains FY 2026 EPS guidance of $8.55 to $9.05 as tax services revenue reaches $96M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pathward Financial, Inc. (CASH) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • “At the midpoint of our fiscal year, we continue to make good progress on our goals and execute on our long-term strategy; being the trusted platform that enables our partners to thrive.” (CEO Brett Pharr)

  • “Our tax season is going very well with tax-related products leading the way in revenue growth for the quarter.” (CEO Pharr) Pharr also said “net interest income from our commercial finance loans also increased significantly,” and added, “we are maintaining our guidance range of $8.55 to $9.05 earnings per diluted share.” (CEO Pharr)

  • “For the 6 months ending March 31, 2026, we increased total tax product revenue by 13%… This brought total Tax Services revenue to $96 million.” (CEO Pharr) He added, “Refund Advance originations increased by over $200 million this year,” and “pre-tax income of $62 million for Tax Services, an increase of 30%.” (CEO Pharr)

  • “In April, after the quarter closed, Pathward executed a 3-year extension with TabaPay, a leading money movement platform.” (CEO Pharr)

  • “Due to the continued backlog from the first government shutdown, we fell short of our goal range for secondary-market revenues, but we believe this is primarily a timing impact, and we expect to make up the difference in subsequent quarters.” (Executive VP & CFO Gregory Sigrist)

Outlook

  • “We are maintaining our guidance range of $8.55 to $9.05 earnings per diluted share.” (CEO Brett Pharr)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management did not introduce a new EPS range and continued to reference the same range of $8.55 to $9.05. (CEO Pharr)

  • On margin direction, “I still continue to believe we’re stable to up slightly trending up on the adjusted net interest margin.” (Executive VP & CFO Gregory Sigrist)

Financial results

  • “We reported net income of $72.9 million and earnings per diluted share of $3.35.” (CEO Brett Pharr)

  • “Noninterest income in the quarter grew 9% and represented 55% of our total revenue.” (CEO Pharr) He also cited seasonality: “these metrics generally hit their high point during this quarter due to the seasonality of the tax business.” (CEO Pharr)

  • “Loans and leases at March 31 grew 9%… with a $588 million increase in our core commercial finance business, particularly in renewable energy and structured finance.” (Executive VP & CFO Gregory Sigrist)

  • “Net interest margin was 6.3% in the quarter. Our adjusted net interest margin was 5.32%.” (CFO Sigrist) He attributed the adjusted NIM improvement vs. the prior-year period to “lower rate-related card expenses.” (CFO Sigrist)

  • “Our liquidity remains strong with $2.7 billion available.” (CFO Sigrist) He also said, “During the quarter, we repurchased approximately 855,000 shares at an average price of $84.15,” leaving “3.4 million shares still available for repurchase.” (CFO Sigrist)

Q&A

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked whether the higher buyback pace was repeatable and about M&A priorities; Executive VP & CFO Sigrist said buybacks are seasonal and “still the highest use of capital,” while CEO Pharr said, “we’ve got a pretty high hurdle rate… we’ve just not seen anything over time that make any sense.”

  • Switzer, KBW: asked if prior commentary on new programs contributing “mid- to high-single-digits” to card fees still held; CFO Sigrist replied, “the guide we gave previously… still applies,” and CEO Pharr said, “we’re now seeing the benefit of that come through the noninterest income line.”

  • Switzer, KBW: asked about a proposed executive order on collecting citizenship info; CEO Pharr said, “we don’t what the rules are,” and added, “we already have the processes in place with our partners to collect the necessary information.”

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: pressed on rising NPAs/NPLs and resolution timing; CFO Sigrist said the 30-59 day bucket “went up by about $40 million” from “a limited number of loans that frankly came current after quarter end,” and that the NPL ratio move was driven by “greater than 90 days past due and still accruing.”

  • Yanchunis, Raymond James: asked about the provision increase; CFO Sigrist said it was “a mix of both” specific reserving and “our normal CECL process,” while emphasizing, “we still believe this is… a very stable credit environment.”

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked for pipeline color and loan growth/NIM direction; CEO Pharr said, “our pipeline is very strong,” and CFO Sigrist said he expects “some modest continued uptick” in loans and reiterated “stable to up slightly trending up” adjusted NIM, citing seasonal borrowing needs in the March quarter.

  • Switzer, KBW: asked about BaaS competition and charters plus stablecoin interest; CEO Pharr said, “everybody is going and getting a bank charter right now,” but added, “we’re not seeing any impact yet,” and on stablecoin: “we’re watching it and participating in the dialogue, but it’s not the kind of thing we’re announcing.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on buybacks/capital allocation, credit metrics, regulatory/citizenship requirements, and competitive threats; questions included “NPAs ticked higher again,” “the provision increased pretty materially,” and “what do you view the biggest potential risk to the industry?” (Raymond James; KBW)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to seasonality and timing; examples included “we expect to make up the difference in subsequent quarters” on secondary-market revenue and “we don’t what the rules are” on potential citizenship requirements. (Executive VP & CFO Sigrist; CEO Brett Pharr)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained an upbeat stance on the “robust” pipeline while fielding more detailed regulatory and competitive questions; in Q1, CEO Pharr said the pipeline “has never been more full,” while in Q2 he emphasized, “we’re not seeing any impact yet” from charter moves. (CEO Pharr)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language stayed consistent: in Q1, management said, “We are increasing our fiscal year 2026 guidance to an EPS range of $8.55 to $9.05,” and in Q2, “we are maintaining our guidance range of $8.55 to $9.05.” (Executive VP & CFO Gregory Sigrist; CEO Brett Pharr)

  • Tax shifted from setup to results: Q1 previewed tax season with “over 11% more enrolled tax offices,” while Q2 reported, “we operated with over 48,000 independent tax offices,” and reported higher 6-month tax revenue and tax pre-tax income. (CEO Pharr)

  • Q&A focus broadened: Q1 concentrated on NIM trajectory, partner ramp, and charter/crypto positioning; Q2 added buyback repeatability, citizenship-information compliance risk, and deeper credit/provision for credit loss discussion. (KBW; Raymond James)

Risks and concerns

  • Secondary-market revenue timing risk: “we fell short of our goal range… primarily a timing impact.” (Executive VP & CFO Gregory Sigrist)

  • Credit monitoring and provisioning: “Our nonperforming loans saw a modest increase to 2.39%,” and the allowance ratio increase was attributed to “a mix of specific reserves and our CECL model.” (CFO Sigrist)

  • Regulatory uncertainty: “Until we know what the rules are, we don’t know what the implications are.” (CEO Brett Pharr)

  • Competitive dynamics and charters: “I do believe there’ll be some that are successful in 2 or 3 years from now… but… we’re not seeing any impact yet.” (CEO Pharr)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a seasonally strong quarter led by Tax Services execution and commercial finance growth, reiterated full-year EPS guidance of $8.55 to $9.05, and emphasized a continued preference for share repurchases as “the highest use of capital,” while acknowledging timing pressure in secondary-market revenue and increased investor attention to credit metrics, regulatory uncertainty, and evolving BaaS competition.
